stem: Mendel published his work on inheritance of characters in 1865, but it remained unrecognised for a long time. Which of the following is the most accurate reason for this?
options: Mendel used garden peas which were not accepted as a model organism by his contemporaries
His concept of discrete, stable factors that did not blend was not consistent with the continuous variation observed in nature
The concept of meiosis was not discovered during Mendel's time, making his laws impossible to verify
Mendel did not perform enough crosses to statistically support his conclusions
subject: Botany

stem: Chromosomes were visualised as 'coloured bodies' because they were identified by a specific technique. Which of the following correctly explains their naming?
options: They are inherently coloured due to the DNA-protein composition
They appeared coloured when examined under sunlight in early microscopes
They were visualised by staining, appearing as coloured bodies under the microscope
They reflect coloured light due to their helical structure
subject: Botany

stem: By which year had chromosome movement during meiosis been completely worked out, enabling Sutton and Boveri to draw parallels with Mendel's laws?
subject: Botany

stem: Walter Sutton and Theodore Boveri united the knowledge of chromosomal segregation with Mendelian principles. What is the name given to this synthesis?
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following correctly describes the location of two alleles of a gene pair on chromosomes, as established by the chromosomal theory of inheritance?
options: Both alleles are located on the same chromosome at the same locus
The two alleles are located on homologous sites on homologous chromosomes
Alleles are located on non-homologous chromosomes at corresponding sites
One allele is on a chromosome and the other floats freely in the nucleus
subject: Botany

stem: Drosophila melanogaster was found to be a very suitable organism for Morgan's genetic studies. Which of the following is NOT a feature that made it suitable?
options: It completes its life cycle in about two weeks
A single mating can produce a large number of progenies
It can be grown on simple synthetic medium in the laboratory
It has only one pair of chromosomes, simplifying genetic analysis
explanation: Drosophila has 4 pairs of chromosomes, not 1.
subject: Botany

stem: Morgan hybridised yellow-bodied, white-eyed females to brown-bodied, red-eyed males in Drosophila and intercrossed their F1 progeny. What result was observed in F2?
options: The two genes segregated independently giving a 9:3:3:1 ratio
The F2 ratio deviated significantly from 9:3:3:1 due to linkage of the two genes
The genes showed complete dominance giving a 3:1 ratio
All F2 offspring showed parental phenotypes only
subject: Botany

stem: Morgan coined the term 'linkage' in the context of his Drosophila experiments. Which of the following is the most precise definition of linkage as used by Morgan?
options: The tendency of genes on different chromosomes to be inherited together
The physical association of two or more genes on the same chromosome
The generation of non-parental gene combinations through crossing over
The failure of homologous chromosomes to separate during meiosis
subject: Botany

stem: Morgan used the term 'recombination' to describe a specific genetic phenomenon. Which of the following best represents this term?
options: The pairing of homologous chromosomes during meiosis I
The physical association of genes present on the same chromosome
The generation of non-parental gene combinations during inheritance
The movement of chromosomes to opposite poles during anaphase II
subject: Botany

stem: In Morgan's dihybrid crosses in Drosophila, when two genes are located on the same chromosome, what was the characteristic observation about parental gene combinations in the offspring?
options: Parental combinations and non-parental combinations appeared in equal proportion
Non-parental gene combinations were much higher than parental combinations
Parental gene combinations were much higher than non-parental combinations
Only parental combinations appeared and recombinants were completely absent
subject: Botany

stem: Morgan found that the genes 'white' and 'yellow' in Drosophila showed only 1.3% recombination, while 'white' and 'miniature wing' showed 37.2% recombination. This observation indicates that:
options: White and yellow are located on different chromosomes
White and miniature wing are more tightly linked than white and yellow
White and yellow are very tightly linked, while white and miniature wing are loosely linked
Recombination frequency has no relationship with the distance between genes
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following best explains the basis of independent assortment during meiosis I?
options: Homologous chromosomes separate during mitotic division
Different homologous chromosome pairs orient independently at the metaphase plate during Metaphase I
Sister chromatids separate randomly during Prophase I
Crossing over prevents homologous chromosomes from pairing together
subject: Botany

stem: In 1900, three scientists independently rediscovered Mendel's results on inheritance. Who were these scientists?
options: de Vries, Correns and von Tschermak
subject: Botany

stem: Just as genes occupy a specific locus on a chromosome, Morgan's linkage data established a parallel principle. Which of the following best describes this parallelism between the behaviour of genes and chromosomes?
options: Genes segregate during meiosis because chromosomes segregate, and linked genes travel together because they reside on the same chromosome
Genes mutate independently of chromosomes and therefore do not follow chromosomal movement during cell division
Linked genes assort independently just as homologous chromosomes assort independently during meiosis I
Chromosomes replicate during S phase but genes replicate only during G2 phase of the cell cycle
subject: Botany

stem: Sutton and Boveri argued that the pairing and separation of a pair of chromosomes would lead to a specific genetic consequence. What was this consequence?
options: Blending of parental characters in the offspring
Segregation of the pair of factors (genes) carried by those chromosomes
Fusion of the two alleles to form a new combined allele
Random mutation of genes during each cell division
subject: Botany

stem: Match Column I with Column II regarding the history of chromosomal theory of inheritance: A) 1865; B) 1900; C) 1902; D) Chromosomes. Column II: P) Chromosome movement during meiosis worked out; Q) Mendel published his work; R) Visualised by staining, appearing as coloured bodies; S) Three scientists independently rediscovered Mendel's results.
subject: Botany

stem: Match scientists with their specific contributions to chromosomal theory / linkage: A) Sutton and Boveri; B) T.H. Morgan; C) de Vries, Correns, von Tschermak; D) Alfred Sturtevant. Column II: P) Rediscovered Mendel's results in 1900; Q) Used recombination frequency to map genes on chromosomes; R) Coined terms linkage and recombination; worked on Drosophila; S) Explained Mendel's laws using chromosome movement.
subject: Botany

stem: Match terms related to linkage and recombination with definitions: A) Linkage; B) Recombination; C) Tight linkage; D) Loose linkage. Column II: P) Higher recombination frequency; genes far apart on same chromosome; Q) Physical association of genes on the same chromosome; R) Very low recombination; genes very close on the same chromosome; S) Generation of non-parental gene combinations.
subject: Botany

stem: Match Column I (features of Drosophila melanogaster) with Column II (their relevance for genetic studies): A) Short life cycle (~2 weeks); B) Large number of progeny from a single mating; C) Clear sex differentiation; D) Many hereditary variations visible under low-power microscope. Column II: P) Allows easy identification of sex-linked traits in crosses; Q) Enables multiple generations to be studied quickly; R) Permits direct observation of genetic diversity; S) Provides large sample size for statistically reliable results.
subject: Botany

stem: Match Column I (gene pairs studied by Morgan in Drosophila) with Column II (their recombination frequencies): A) white and yellow; B) white and miniature wing; C) Genes on different chromosomes; D) Genes at same locus on same chromosome. Column II: P) 50% recombination (independent assortment); Q) 0% recombination (completely allelic, not separate loci); R) 37.2% recombination (loosely linked); S) 1.3% recombination (tightly linked).
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Mendel's concept of genes as stable and discrete units that control trait expression was not accepted by his contemporaries. Statement II: Biologists of Mendel's era believed in continuous blending of traits as the mechanism of inheritance.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Mendel's use of mathematics to explain biological phenomena was novel and was readily accepted by all biologists of his time. Statement II: Mendel could not provide any physical proof for the existence of factors or state what they were made of.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Chromosomes occur in pairs, and the two alleles of a gene pair are located on homologous sites on homologous chromosomes. Statement II: Genes occur in pairs, and each member of a gene pair segregates into a different gamete during gamete formation.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: In Drosophila dihybrid crosses studied by Morgan, when two genes were situated on the same chromosome, non-parental type combinations were much higher than parental type combinations. Statement II: Genes located on the same chromosome show physical association and are said to be linked.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Morgan found that yellow-bodied, white-eyed flies and brown-bodied, red-eyed flies were the parental combinations that appeared in much higher frequency in F2. Statement II: The higher frequency of parental combinations in F2 was attributed by Morgan to the physical association (linkage) of yellow and white genes on the X chromosome.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Drosophila melanogaster is maintained in the laboratory on complex natural medium requiring special temperature-controlled conditions. Statement II: Drosophila melanogaster shows a clear differentiation of sexes, with male and female flies being easily distinguishable.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Statement I: Higher recombination frequency between two genes indicates that they are loosely linked and situated farther apart on the same chromosome. Statement II: The genes white and miniature wing in Drosophila show higher recombination than the genes white and yellow, indicating white and miniature wing are farther apart.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Consider the following statements regarding the reasons for Mendel's work remaining unrecognised till 1900: (i) Communication was not easy and his work could not be widely publicised; (ii) His concept of non-blending discrete factors was not consistent with continuous variation seen in nature; (iii) His use of mathematics to explain biology was totally new and unacceptable to many biologists; (iv) Mendel could not provide physical proof for the existence of factors or their chemical composition. Select the correct statements.
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements correctly describe the behaviour parallels between chromosomes and genes that were noted by Sutton and Boveri? (i) Both chromosomes and genes occur in pairs; (ii) Both segregate at gamete formation such that only one of each pair is transmitted to a gamete; (iii) Independent pairs of chromosomes segregate independently of each other, just as independent gene pairs do; (iv) Both chromosomes and genes are made exclusively of protein.
subject: Botany

stem: Identify the correct statements regarding Drosophila melanogaster as a model organism for genetics: (i) Drosophila can be grown on simple synthetic medium in the laboratory; (ii) Its life cycle is completed in about two weeks; (iii) A single mating produces a large number of progeny; (iv) It has many types of hereditary variations visible under low-power microscopes.
options: (i), (ii) and (iv) are correct, but (iii) is incorrect
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements correctly describe Morgan's findings regarding linkage and recombination in Drosophila? (i) Genes situated on the same chromosome showed higher frequency of parental combinations than non-parental combinations; (ii) All genes on the same chromosome showed identical recombination frequencies; (iii) Some genes were very tightly linked (very low recombination) while others were loosely linked (higher recombination); (iv) Morgan coined the term linkage for physical association of genes on a chromosome.
subject: Botany

stem: Select the correct statements about the independent assortment of chromosomes during meiosis: (i) Different alleles of a gene segregate during Anaphase of meiosis I; (ii) The independent alignment of chromosome pairs is the physical basis of Mendel's law of independent assortment; (iii) Independent assortment only applies when genes are located on the same chromosome; (iv) Chromosomes of a pair segregate such that only one of each pair is transmitted to a gamete.
subject: Botany

stem: Consider the following statements regarding Morgan's conclusions on linkage in Drosophila: (i) Morgan attributed the deviation from 9:3:3:1 ratio to the physical association of two genes located on the same chromosome; (ii) All genes present on the same chromosome show equal and identical degrees of linkage with each other; (iii) Tightly linked genes are located very close to each other on the same chromosome and show very low recombination frequency; (iv) The degree of linkage between two genes depends on the distance between their loci on the chromosome.
subject: Botany

stem: Assertion (A): Mendel's rediscovery in 1900 by de Vries, Correns and von Tschermak coincided with significant advances in understanding cell division. Reason (R): By 1902, scientists using improved microscopy had worked out chromosome movement during meiosis, enabling a chromosomal explanation of Mendel's laws.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Assertion (A): When two genes are located on the same chromosome, the proportion of parental gene combinations in F2 offspring is much higher than non-parental combinations. Reason (R): Genes on the same chromosome are physically associated (linked) and tend to be transmitted together into the same gamete, restricting free recombination.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Assertion (A): Morgan's dihybrid cross in Drosophila involving yellow-body/white-eye and brown-body/red-eye genes did not produce a 9:3:3:1 F2 ratio. Reason (R): The two genes involved were located on the X chromosome (sex-linked) and were linked to each other, causing deviation from the expected independent assortment ratio.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
subject: Botany

stem: Assertion (A): Tightly linked genes such as white and yellow in Drosophila (1.3% recombination) are considered to be located very close together on the same chromosome. Reason (R): The frequency of recombination between genes on the same chromosome is directly proportional to the physical distance between them; lower recombination frequency indicates greater physical distance.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: Lower recombination frequency = SMALLER physical distance, not greater.
subject: Botany

stem: Assertion (A): Independent assortment of genes, as described by Mendel, has a physical basis in the independent alignment of non-homologous chromosome pairs at the metaphase plate during meiosis I. Reason (R): Genes located on the same chromosome always show independent assortment because they align independently during meiosis I.
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: Genes on the same chromosome show LINKAGE, not independent assortment.
subject: Botany

stem: Arrange the following events in the correct chronological sequence related to the development of the chromosomal theory of inheritance: (P) Sutton and Boveri noted parallels between chromosome behaviour and Mendel's laws and proposed the chromosomal theory; (Q) Mendel published his work on inheritance of characters; (R) Three scientists (de Vries, Correns, von Tschermak) independently rediscovered Mendel's results; chromosome movement during meiosis was worked out; (S) Morgan experimentally verified the chromosomal theory and discovered the basis of linkage and recombination in Drosophila.
subject: Botany

stem: Arrange the following events in the correct sequence as they occur during meiosis I to demonstrate independent assortment of two chromosome pairs: (P) Chromosomes segregate to opposite poles during anaphase I, with each pair moving independently; (Q) Homologous chromosomes pair up during prophase I (synapsis); (R) Two chromosome pairs align at the metaphase plate independently of each other during metaphase I; (S) Haploid cells are formed, each receiving one chromosome from each pair.
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements about the reasons for delayed recognition of Mendel's work is NOT correct?
options: Communication was poor and Mendel's work could not be widely publicized
Mendel's concept of non-blending discrete factors did not explain the continuous variation observed in nature
Mendel used mathematics to explain biological phenomena, which was totally new to many biologists of his time
Mendel's work was rejected because he failed to observe the law of segregation in his experiments
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements regarding the chromosomal theory of inheritance is NOT correct?
options: Sutton and Boveri noted that the behaviour of chromosomes was parallel to the behaviour of genes
The chromosomal theory of inheritance was proposed based on the parallels between chromosome movement and Mendelian principles
The chromosomal theory of inheritance was experimentally verified by Thomas Hunt Morgan and his colleagues
Walter Sutton alone proposed the chromosomal theory without any contribution from Boveri
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements about the characteristics of Drosophila melanogaster as a genetic model organism is NOT correct?
options: Drosophila can be grown on simple synthetic medium in the laboratory
Drosophila completes its life cycle in about two weeks
A single mating of Drosophila can produce a large number of progeny flies
The sexes of Drosophila are indistinguishable and require chromosomal analysis for identification
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements about linkage and recombination as described by Morgan is NOT correct?
options: Linkage refers to the physical association of genes on the same chromosome
Recombination refers to the generation of non-parental gene combinations
When two genes are on the same chromosome, parental gene combinations appear in higher proportion than non-parental type
All genes located on the same chromosome show exactly the same recombination frequency regardless of their relative positions
subject: Botany

stem: Which of the following statements about the relationship between chromosome behaviour and Mendel's laws as noted by Sutton and Boveri is NOT correct?
options: Chromosomes occur in pairs, just as genes occur in pairs
Chromosomes segregate at gamete formation so that only one of each pair enters a gamete, mirroring gene segregation
Independent pairs of chromosomes segregate independently, providing the physical basis for independent assortment of genes
The pairing of homologous chromosomes during meiosis leads to blending of the genes they carry into new combined forms
subject: Botany

stem: Five factors affect the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. Which of the following is NOT one of those factors?
subject: Zoology

stem: For the ABO-blood group system, the frequencies of A and B alleles are 0.8 and 0.2, respectively. The expected frequency of AB-blood group bearing organisms is likely to be
subject: Zoology

stem: A population of finches shows variation in beak size. In a habitat where large, hard seeds are the most abundant food source, finches with large beaks survive and reproduce successfully, whereas finches with intermediate and small beaks suffer higher mortality. This pattern of natural selection is an example of:
subject: Zoology

stem: A small group of finches was accidentally swept to a remote island by a storm, where they established a new population. Over generations, this island population showed significantly different allele frequencies compared to the mainland population. This scenario best illustrates which of the following evolutionary mechanisms?
subject: Zoology

stem: Match the types of selection in Column I with their characteristic features in Column II. Column I: A) Stabilising selection; B) Directional selection; C) Disruptive selection; D) Balancing selection. Column II: i) Individuals with traits away from the mean are favoured; ii) Individuals with one extreme phenotype are favoured; iii) Heterozygote individuals have a higher rate of survival; iv) Individuals with the average phenotype are favoured.
subject: Zoology

stem: At a locus, the frequency of allele A = 0.6 and allele a = 0.4. What is the frequency of heterozygotes (Aa) in a random mating population at equilibrium?
subject: Zoology

stem: According to Darwin's theory of natural selection, evolution occurs because:
options: Organisms acquire useful characters during their lifetime and transmit them to offspring
Sudden mutations alone give rise to new species
Individuals with favourable heritable variations survive and reproduce more successfully
Evolution proceeds through large discontinuous changes
subject: Zoology

stem: Read the following statements based on Darwin's theory of Natural Selection. (a) Natural resources are limited and population sizes remain stable except for seasonal fluctuations; (b) Darwin proposed that variations that make resource utilisation better will enable only those individuals to reproduce and leave more progeny; (c) According to Darwin, all variations arising in a population — whether heritable or non-heritable — contribute equally to evolution; (d) The work of Thomas Malthus on populations influenced Darwin's concept of competition for resources. How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: a, b, d are correct; c is wrong — only heritable variations matter.
subject: Zoology

stem: In a population at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the frequency of the homozygous dominant genotype is 0.36. What are the frequencies of dominant and recessive alleles for the locus?
subject: Zoology

stem: A gene locus has two alleles A, a. If the frequency of dominant allele A is 0.4, then what will be the frequency of homozygous dominant, heterozygous and homozygous recessive individuals in the population?
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following serves as an indicator of atmospheric pollution?
subject: Zoology

stem: Which one of the following factors does not affect Hardy-Weinberg's equilibrium?
explanation: Random mating is REQUIRED for HW equilibrium; it doesn't disturb it.
subject: Zoology

stem: In the case of peppered moths (Biston betularia), the black-coloured form became dominant over the light-coloured form in England during the Industrial Revolution. This is an example of
options: Appearance of darker coloured individuals due to very poor sunlight
Inheritance of a darker colour character acquired due to the darker environment
Natural selection, whereby the darker forms were selected
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following explains the Hardy-Weinberg principle?
options: New species arise by sudden variations
A small isolated population forms a new species
Animals tend to increase in size during evolution
Gene pool of a non-evolving population remains constant
subject: Zoology

stem: Statement I: A deviation from the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium indicates that evolutionary forces are acting on the population. Statement II: Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the total of all allelic frequencies keeps on changing, but still the sum remains equal to one. Which of the following options is correct?
options: Both statements I and II are correct
Both statements I and II are incorrect
Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect and Statement II is correct
subject: Zoology

stem: A few individuals from a large population become isolated and establish a new population. Because they carry only a small sample of the original gene pool, the allele frequencies of the new population differ from those of the parent population. This phenomenon is known as:
subject: Zoology

stem: Individuals showing extreme (peripheral) character values at both ends of a distribution curve are favoured in which type of natural selection?
subject: Zoology

stem: According to the Hardy-Weinberg principle, a discrepancy between observed (measured) and expected allelic frequencies indicates all of the following, except —
options: Changes occurring in the gene pool
The extent to which evolutionary change has taken place
subject: Zoology

stem: Genetic drift operates in
subject: Zoology

stem: Birds with average-sized wings survived a severe storm more successfully than with longer or shorter wings. This illustrates:
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Darwinian variations are small and nondirectional
According to Darwin, any population has built-in variation in characteristics
Gene flow occurs among populations of different species
subject: Zoology

stem: In a species, the weight of newborns ranges from 2 to 5 kg. 97% of the newborns with an average weight between 3 to 3.3 kg survive whereas 99% of the infants born with weight from 2 to 2.5 kg or 4.5 to 5 kg die. Which type of selection process is taking place?
subject: Zoology

stem: Variation in gene frequencies within populations can occur by chance rather than by natural selection. This random change in gene frequency is called:
subject: Zoology

stem: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is an example of which of the following?
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following factors will disrupt a population that is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
options: Maintaining a very large population size
No migration of new alleles into the population
subject: Zoology

stem: Artificial selection to obtain cows yielding higher milk output represents
options: Directional, as it pushes the mean of the character in one direction
Disruptive, as it splits the population into two — one yielding higher output and the other lower output
Stabilising followed by disruptive, as it stabilizes the population to produce higher-yielding cows
Stabilising selection, as it stabilizes this character in the population
subject: Zoology

stem: Appearance of dark-coloured peppered moths among the light-coloured ones as a result of increased industrial pollution is an example of
subject: Zoology

stem: Hugo de Vries, while working on Oenothera lamarckiana, proposed that new species arise not through gradual accumulation of small variations, but through sudden, large heritable changes. He termed this mechanism:
subject: Zoology

stem: Genetic drift is a ____ process.
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following concepts is attributed to Charles Darwin?
options: Use and disuse of organs are of great importance in the evolution
Every cell must come from a pre-existing cell
In the struggle for existence, the fittest would survive
The gametes will carry only one of a pair of contrasting characters
subject: Zoology

stem: Select the incorrect match with respect to the scientist and the key postulate of his theory of evolution
options: Malthus — Use and disuse of organs
explanation: 'Use and disuse of organs' is Lamarck, not Malthus. Malthus = population growth / competition.
subject: Zoology

stem: What was Thomas Malthus's work that influenced Darwin?
options: An essay on the principles of Population
subject: Zoology

stem: Which of the following was not explained by Darwinism?
options: Causes of variations among members of a population
explanation: Darwin could not explain the CAUSES of variations — that came later from Mendel.
subject: Zoology

stem: What is convergent evolution?
options: The process by which one species evolves into another over time
The process by which a species becomes better adapted to its environment through natural selection
The process by which different species evolve similar traits in response to similar environmental pressures
The process by which genetic variation is introduced into a population through mutation
subject: Zoology

stem: Assertion (A): Directional selection operates in response to gradual changes in environmental conditions, shifting the mean phenotype of a population towards one extreme over successive generations. Reason (R): Stabilizing selection operates when the intermediate phenotype coincides with the optimal environmental conditions, thereby reducing phenotypic variance without shifting the mean.
options: Both A and R are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion
Both A and R are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Both Assertion and Reason are false
subject: Zoology

stem: In the Hardy-Weinberg algebraic equation $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$, what does 2pq represent?
subject: Zoology

stem: Read the statements (i–iv) and choose the correct option. (i) Increase in melanised moths after industrialisation in Great Britain is an evidence of Natural Selection; (ii) More individuals acquiring the mean character value cause disruption; (iii) Change in allelic frequency leads to the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium; (iv) Genetic drift randomly changes allelic frequency in future generations.
subject: Zoology

stem: Which one of the following scientists' names is correctly matched with the theory put forth by him?
options: Alfred Wallace — Theory of Use and Disuse of Organs
Louis Pasteur — Theory of special creation
Darwin — Theory of Continuity of Germplasm
subject: Zoology

stem: All the following statements about Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium are correct except
options: Any change in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium indicates evolution
Gene flow, genetic drift, mutation, genetic recombination and natural selection are five factors that affect this equilibrium
Random mating always induces a change in this genetic equilibrium
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium allows us to calculate and predict genotype frequencies in large populations
explanation: Random mating MAINTAINS HW equilibrium; non-random mating disturbs it.
subject: Zoology

stem: The rate of appearance of new forms during evolution is linked to:
options: The life cycle generation time of the organism
subject: Zoology

stem: French naturalist Jean-Baptiste de Lamarck said that the evolution of life forms had occurred, but was driven by:
subject: Zoology

stem: The Mutation Theory of evolution was proposed by:
subject: Zoology

stem: According to Darwin, variations are:
subject: Zoology

stem: The total number of genes and their alleles in a population is called:
subject: Zoology

stem: When migration of a section of population to another place occurs, gene frequencies change in the original as well as in the new population. If this gene migration happens multiple times, it is called:
subject: Zoology

stem: The drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor is $v$ when a current $i$ is flowing in it. If both the radius and current are doubled, then the drift velocity will be:
explanation: $v_d = I/(nAe)$; doubling I → ×2, doubling r → A×4, net v/2.
subject: Physics

stem: The resistivity of iron is $1 \times 10^{-7}$ ohm-m. The resistance of an iron wire of particular length and thickness is 1 ohm. If the length and diameter of the wire both are doubled, then the resistivity in ohm-m will be:
explanation: Resistivity is a material property — independent of dimensions.
subject: Physics

stem: Drift velocity $v_d$ varies with the intensity of the electric field as per the relation:
subject: Physics

stem: The resistivity of a wire is $\rho$, its volume is $3\ \text{m}^3$ and its resistance is 3 ohms. Then its length will be:
explanation: $V=AL=3$, $R=\rho L/A=\rho L^2/3=3 \Rightarrow L=3/\sqrt{\rho}$.
subject: Physics

stem: In the circuit shown, what is the value of the unknown resistor R so that the total resistance of the circuit between points P and Q is also equal to R?
subject: Physics

stem: The electric intensity $E$, current density $j$ and resistivity $k$ are related to each other by the relation:
explanation: $E = \rho j = kj$.
subject: Physics

stem: The resistance of a wire of uniform diameter $d$ and length $L$ is $R$. The resistance of another wire of the same material but diameter $2d$ and length $4L$ will be:
explanation: $R'=\rho(4L)/(\pi d^2)=R$ (compare with original $R=\rho L/(\pi d^2/4)$).
subject: Physics

stem: The effective resistance between A and B in the given circuit is (three 6Ω resistors arranged as shown)
explanation: Three 6Ω in parallel = 2Ω.
subject: Physics

stem: If $n$, $e$, $\tau$ and $m$ respectively represent the density, charge, relaxation time and mass of the electron, then the resistance of a wire of length $l$ and area of cross-section $A$ will be:
subject: Physics

stem: Resistance of tungsten wire at 150°C is 133 Ω. Its temperature coefficient of resistance is 0.0045/°C. The resistance of this wire at 500°C will be
subject: Physics

stem: A steady current flows in a metallic conductor of non-uniform cross-section. The quantity/quantities constant along the length of the conductor is/are:
options: Current, electric field and drift speed
subject: Physics

stem: A wire of radius $r$ has resistance R. If it is stretched to a radius of $3r/4$, its resistance becomes
subject: Physics

stem: In the figure, current through the 3Ω resistor is 0.8 ampere, then the potential drop through the 4Ω resistor is
explanation: V across 3Ω||6Ω = 2.4V → I through 4Ω = 1.2A → V = 4.8V.
subject: Physics

stem: What is the equivalent resistance between A and B in the network shown (with two 2R resistors and an R as shown)?
subject: Physics

stem: A wire of resistance R is cut into 10 equal parts perpendicular to its length. These parts are connected in parallel; the equivalent resistance of such connection will be:
explanation: Each part R/10; ten in parallel → R/100.
subject: Physics

stem: Three resistors each of 2 ohm are connected together in a triangular shape. The resistance between any two vertices will be
subject: Physics

stem: There are $n$ similar conductors each of resistance R. The resultant resistance comes out to be $x$ when connected in parallel. If they are connected in series, the resistance comes out to be:
explanation: $R=nx$; series = $nR=n^2 x$.
subject: Physics

stem: Equivalent resistance between A and B will be (balanced Wheatstone-like network of 3Ω arms)
explanation: Balanced Wheatstone — diagonal carries no current — (3+3)||(3+3)=3Ω.
subject: Physics

stem: Referring to the figure below, the effective resistance of the network is (four $r$ resistors arranged as shown)
subject: Physics

stem: Two resistors are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel. The equivalent resistance in the two cases is 9 ohm and 2 ohms respectively. Then the resistances of the component resistors are:
subject: Physics

stem: The equivalent resistance of the arrangement of resistances shown in the adjoining figure between the points A and B is (8Ω, 16Ω, 16Ω, 20Ω, 9Ω, 18Ω, 6Ω network)
subject: Physics

stem: Four resistances (4Ω, 6Ω, 4Ω, 6Ω) are connected in a circuit with a 20V source as shown. The electric current flowing through 4 ohm and 6 ohm resistance is respectively:
explanation: Two branches of 10Ω in parallel across 20V → 2A each.
subject: Physics

stem: In the figure given below, the current passing through the 6Ω resistor is (1.2 A divides between 6Ω and 4Ω in parallel)
subject: Physics

stem: Which of the following combinations of focal lengths for objective ($f_o$) and eyepiece ($f_e$) can be used to design an astronomical telescope? (a) $f_o=50$ cm, $f_e=100$ cm; (b) $f_o=50$ cm, $f_e=50$ cm; (c) $f_o=105$ cm, $f_e=5$ cm; (d) $f_o=2$ cm, $f_e=4$ cm.
explanation: Telescope requires $f_o > f_e$ — only (c) qualifies.
subject: Physics

stem: If four convex lenses are given to you with focal lengths $f_1 < f_2 < f_3 < f_4$, then which of the following combinations will you prefer to form a compound microscope?
explanation: Microscope wants smallest $f_o$ AND small $f_e$ — $f_1$ and $f_2$.
subject: Physics

stem: The magnification of a compound microscope for the final image at least distance of distinct vision is 90. The magnification of the objective lens is 30. The value of the focal length of the eyepiece will be
subject: Physics

stem: For a tiny needle, its image formed by a compound microscope is
subject: Physics

stem: The linear magnification and angular magnification in the microscope have the same magnitude when the image is at a distance of:
subject: Physics

stem: Consider the following four statements concerning a compound microscope: A) Each lens produces an image that is virtual and inverted; B) The objective lens has a very short focal length; C) The eyepiece is used as a magnifying glass; D) The objective lens is convex and the eyepiece is concave. Which two of the four statements are correct?
subject: Physics

stem: Which of the following is NOT correct for a magnifying glass?
options: The image is larger than the object
The object is inside the focal point
explanation: A magnifying glass uses a convex (converging) lens.
subject: Physics

stem: A man uses a convex lens of focal length 5 cm as a magnifying glass. The least distance of distinct vision of the man is 25 cm. Calculate the magnification if the image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision.
subject: Physics

stem: An astronomical telescope uses two converging lenses. Choose the correct response regarding the nature of image observed.
subject: Physics

stem: Five convex lenses are available in the lab — P=2 cm, Q=8 mm, R=10 cm, S=25 cm, T=80 cm. Choose the correct statement(s): A) Compound microscope of highest magnifying power in normal adjustment should use lens P as objective and lens Q as eyepiece; B) Any of the above lens can be used as a magnifying glass in normal adjustment; C) To construct an astronomical telescope of best magnifying power for an unaccommodated eye, lenses Q and T should be used; D) A terrestrial telescope can be designed using lenses P, R and S.
subject: Physics

stem: In the movie Peddi, Ram Charan discovers a mysterious miniature book written by Prof. Cool. To read the tiny letters clearly, he uses a magnifier of focal length 10 cm. The magnifier forms an erect image of the letters at a distance of 20 cm from the lens. The distance of the miniature book from the magnifier is (use Cartesian sign convention, $\dfrac{1}{f}=\dfrac{1}{v}-\dfrac{1}{u}$):
subject: Physics

stem: An object is first seen in red light and then in violet light through a simple microscope. In which case is the magnifying power more?
explanation: Higher refractive index for violet → shorter focal length → larger M.
subject: Physics

stem: Modern telescopes use mirrors, rather than lenses, to form images. One advantage of mirrors over lenses is that the images formed by mirrors are not affected by:
subject: Physics

stem: An astronomical telescope has an angular magnification of magnitude 5 for distant objects. The separation between the objective and the eyepiece is 60 cm. The final image is formed at infinity. The focal lengths $f_o$ of the objective and $f_e$ of the eyepiece are
subject: Physics

stem: A simple telescope, consisting of an objective of focal length 100 cm and a single eye lens of focal length 5 cm, is focused on a distant object in such a way that the final image is formed at infinity. If the object subtends an angle of 30' (30 min) at the objective, the angular width of the image is
subject: Physics

stem: The angle subtended by a distant object at the objective of a telescope is 0.1 radian. If the focal length of objective and eyepiece of the telescope is 120 cm and 20 cm, then the size of the image formed by the objective is approximately
explanation: Size $=f_o\theta=120\times 0.1=12$ cm.
subject: Physics

stem: A telescope has objective focal length 100 cm and eyepiece focal length 5 cm. A convex lens of focal length 20 cm is inserted midway between them with its axis coincident with the telescope axis. What is the new angular magnification?
options: Depends on the exact position of the inserted lens
subject: Physics

stem: A compound microscope produces an intermediate image 20 cm from the objective. If the objective focal length is 1 cm, the objective magnification is approximately:
explanation: $1/v-1/u=1/f$ gives $u\approx-1.05$ cm, $m_o\approx 19 \approx 20$.
subject: Physics

stem: The objective of a telescope is covered by an opaque screen leaving only one-fourth of its area exposed. Which of the following changes?
explanation: Reducing aperture cuts light gathered (brightness) but not the angular magnification.
subject: Physics

stem: An astronomical telescope is adjusted for normal vision. If the focal length of eyepiece is reduced by 20% while objective remains unchanged, the percentage increase in magnifying power is:
subject: Physics

stem: A telescope of length 120 cm has an eyepiece of focal length 6 cm. The eyepiece is moved 1 cm towards the objective from normal adjustment. The final image will be:
options: At principal focus of the eyepiece
subject: Physics

stem: In an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, the final image is formed at:
subject: Physics

stem: The equation representing the process by which standard reduction potential of zinc can be defined is
subject: Chemistry

stem: $E°_{cell}$ of the reaction $Zn(s)+Cu^{2+}(aq) \to Zn^{2+}(aq)+Cu(s)$ is [If $E°_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V, $E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V]
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is not considered as oxidation?
explanation: Addition of an electropositive element is REDUCTION (gain of electron density).
subject: Chemistry

stem: The correct representation of $HAuCl_4$ in STOCK notation is
explanation: Au oxidation state = $+3$.
subject: Chemistry

stem: In which of the following compounds does nitrogen exhibit the highest oxidation state?
explanation: Average N in $HN_3$ is $-1/3$, higher than $-3$ ($NH_3$), $-2$ ($N_2H_4$), $-1$ ($NH_2OH$).
subject: Chemistry

stem: The function of salt bridge in an electrochemical cell is
options: To prevent the mixing of the electrolyte in two half cells
To maintain the electrical neutrality of the two half cells
subject: Chemistry

stem: The more positive the value of $E°$, the greater is the tendency of the species to get reduced. Using the standard electrode potential of redox couples given below, find out which of the following is the strongest oxidising agent. $E°$ value: $Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}=+0.77$ V; $I_2(s)/I^-=+0.54$ V; $Cu^{2+}/Cu=+0.34$ V; $Ag^+/Ag=+0.80$ V.
explanation: Highest $E°$ → strongest oxidiser. $Ag^+$ (0.80 V) wins.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Assertion (A): $Li^+$ ion is weakest oxidizing agent while Li metal is most powerful reducing agent in aqueous solution. Reason (R): Lithium has lowest standard electrode potential in the electrochemical series ($E°_{Li^+/Li} = -3.05$ V).
options: Both A and R are true and R is a correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
subject: Chemistry

stem: In which of the following compounds is the oxidation of iodine fractional?
explanation: In $I_3^-$ the average oxidation state of I is $-1/3$.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Identify the average oxidation state of Fe in $Fe_3O_4$
explanation: $3x+4(-2)=0 \Rightarrow x=8/3$.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $2IO_3^- + xI^- + 12H^+ \to 6I_2 + 6H_2O$. What is the value of $x$?
explanation: Iodine balance: $2+x=12 \Rightarrow x=10$.
subject: Chemistry

stem: The order of increasing oxidation numbers of S in $S_8, S_2O_8^{2-}, S_2O_3^{2-}, S_4O_6^{2-}$ is given below.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following reactions does NOT involve oxidation–reduction?
explanation: Option 3 is acid–base/metathesis; no oxidation state changes.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $I^- + ClO_3^- + H_2SO_4 \to Cl^- + HSO_4^- + I_2$. The incorrect statement in the balanced equation is
explanation: S remains in $+6$ throughout (in both $H_2SO_4$ and $HSO_4^-$).
subject: Chemistry

stem: $NO_2 + xH^+ + ye^- \to NH_2 + 2H_2O$. Find the sum of $(x + y)$ in the above reaction.
explanation: Balance: H gives $x=6$; charge balance gives $y=6$ → $x+y=12$.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is NOT an example of redox reaction?
explanation: Option 4 is metathesis — no change in oxidation states.
subject: Chemistry

stem: In case of peroxides and superoxides, oxidation number of oxygen respectively are
subject: Chemistry

stem: In oxygen difluoride ($OF_2$) and dioxygen difluoride ($O_2F_2$), the oxygen is assigned an oxidation number of
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is a disproportionation reaction?
explanation: N in $NO_2$ (+4) goes to both $NO_3^-$ (+5, oxidised) and $NO_2^-$ (+3, reduced).
subject: Chemistry

stem: In the following redox reaction, $xFe^{2+} + yCr_2O_7^{2-} + zH^+ \to Fe^{3+} + Cr^{3+} + H_2O$. $x$, $y$ and $z$ are respectively
subject: Chemistry

stem: The oxidation states of phosphorus vary from
subject: Chemistry

stem: For the redox reaction $MnO_4^- + C_2O_4^{2-} + H^+ \to Mn^{2+} + CO_2 + H_2O$, the correct coefficients of the reactants for the balanced reaction are $MnO_4^-, C_2O_4^{2-}, H^+$ as:
subject: Chemistry

stem: In the process $NO_2^- \to NH_3$, the number of $OH^-$ ions and the side on which they should be added in balancing are
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which one of the following is incorrect about $CHCl_3$?
options: The major use of chloroform today is in the production of the freon refrigerant R-22
Chronic exposure may cause damage of liver (where it is metabolised to phosgene) and to the kidneys
Breathing about 900 ppm of chloroform helps to relieve dizziness, fatigue, and headache

Chloroform is slowly oxidised by air in the presence of light to an extremely poisonous gas called carbonyl chloride or phosgene; it is therefore stored in closed dark-coloured bottles completely filled so that air is kept out: $2CHCl_3 + O_2 \to 2COCl_2 + 2HCl$
explanation: 900 ppm chloroform causes those symptoms — does not relieve them.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which one of them is an incorrect match?
explanation: Diethyl ether ($C_2H_5OC_2H_5$) is symmetrical, not unsymmetrical.
subject: Chemistry

stem: The order of C–O bond angle in A, B and C (water, diphenyl ether, dimethyl ether) is
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following statement is correct?
options: Chloroform is stored in dark colour bottles
Chloroform is oxidised by air in light to phosgene (carbonyl chloride), which is extremely poisonous
Antiseptic property of iodoform is due to the liberation of free iodine, not due to iodoform itself
subject: Chemistry

stem: Phenol is less acidic than ____
explanation: o-Nitrophenol is more acidic than phenol due to the electron-withdrawing $NO_2$ group.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Assertion (A): Increasing order of acid strength of alcohol is 1° alcohol > 2° alcohol > 3° alcohol. Reason (R): An electron-releasing group increases the electron density on oxygen, which tends to decrease the polarity of the O–H bond.
options: Both A and R are true and R is a correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is NOT a correct match?
explanation: Phenol is NOT an allylic alcohol — it's a phenolic compound (OH directly on aromatic ring).
subject: Chemistry

stem: A benzene ring with three –OH groups, and $CH_2OH{-}CHOH{-}CH_2OH$ (glycerol) can be classified respectively as?
options: Trihydric phenol and Trihydric alcohol respectively
Trihydric alcohol and Trihydric phenol respectively
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is an incorrect match?
explanation: Butane-2-ol is sec-butyl alcohol. Tert-butyl alcohol is 2-methylpropan-2-ol.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Hydroquinone is?
subject: Chemistry

stem: Match the columns. Column I (IUPAC name): A) 3-Methylphenol; B) Benzene-1,4-diol; C) Benzene-1,2-diol; D) Benzene-1,3-diol. Column II (Common name): i) Catechol; ii) Resorcinol; iii) Hydroquinone or Quinol; iv) Quinone; v) m-cresol; vi) Xylenol.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $CH_3{-}C(=O){-}CH_3 + Ph{-}MgBr$ (dry ether) → Adduct → (Hydrolysis) → Alcohol. The Adduct is?
explanation: Grignard adds to the C=O carbon; O picks up MgBr before hydrolysis.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $Ph{-}CH_2{-}CH{=}CH{-}CH_2{-}CHO$ → $Ph{-}CH_2{-}CH{=}CH{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}OH$. The suitable reagent for this reaction is?
explanation: $NaBH_4$ selectively reduces aldehyde to alcohol without reducing the C=C bond.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $CH_3{-}CH{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow{B_2H_6/THF} A \xrightarrow{H_2O_2/OH^-} B \text{ (Major)}$. A and B are respectively?
explanation: Hydroboration–oxidation gives anti-Markovnikov OH at the terminal carbon.
subject: Chemistry

stem: $CH_3{-}CH{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow{(1)\ B_2H_6/THF,\ (2)\ H_2{}^{18}O_2/OH^-} ?$ Major (where the peroxide oxygen carries the $^{18}O$ isotope label).
explanation: Anti-Markovnikov + OH oxygen originates from $H_2O_2$ ($^{18}O$).
subject: Chemistry

stem: $CH_3{-}CH{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+} A$;  $CH_3{-}CH{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow{(1)\ B_2H_6/THF,\ (2)\ H_2O_2/OH^-} B$. A and B are respectively?
explanation: Acid hydration = Markovnikov (2° OH); hydroboration–oxidation = anti-Markovnikov (1° OH).
subject: Chemistry

stem: Alkene treated with water in the presence of acid as catalyst forms an alcohol. In this reaction the 1st step is protonation of the alkene, which can be best represented as
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following statements are true? (I) Aldehyde can be reduced to primary alcohol by using $H_2/Pd$ or $LiAlH_4$ or $NaBH_4$; (II) Ketone can be reduced to secondary alcohol by using $H_2/Pd$ or $LiAlH_4$ or $NaBH_4$; (III) Carboxylic acid can be reduced to primary alcohol by $NaBH_4$; (IV) Carboxylic acid can be reduced to primary alcohol by $LiAlH_4$ in excellent yield.
explanation: $NaBH_4$ does NOT reduce carboxylic acids; everything else is correct.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Why is $LiAlH_4$ used to prepare special chemicals only?
explanation: $LiAlH_4$ gives excellent yields but is expensive (and reacts violently with water), so reserved for special syntheses.
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following statement is true?
options: All aldehydes react with Grignard's reagent followed by hydrolysis to produce a primary alcohol
All ketones react with Grignard's reagent followed by hydrolysis to produce a secondary alcohol
Reaction of Grignard reagent with methanal followed by hydrolysis produces primary alcohol, with aldehydes other than methanal secondary alcohol, and with ketones tertiary alcohol
subject: Chemistry

stem: Which of the following is true about the hydroboration–oxidation reaction?
options: 3 moles of $OH^-$ are required (no $H_2O_2$) to prepare 3 moles of alcohol
3 moles of $H_2O_2$ are required in the presence of $OH^-$ to prepare 3 moles of alcohol
$3/2$ moles of $OH^-$ are required (no $H_2O_2$) to prepare 3 moles of alcohol
$3/2$ moles of $H_2O_2$ are required in the presence of $OH^-$ to prepare 3 moles of alcohol
subject: Chemistry

stem: $\alpha$-Methylstyrene $Ph{-}C(CH_3){=}CH_2$ on hydration ($H_2O/H^+$) gives a major product. Which is the major product?
explanation: Markovnikov + benzylic carbocation stability → OH on the C bearing Ph.
subject: Chemistry
